comment_text.sn35.63:1.4: “One who lives alone” (ekavihārī) is also mentioned at SN 21.10:1.5 and Thag 10.2.
comment_text.sn35.63:1.5: “Partner” is dutiya (“second”), a companion or spouse.
comment_text.sn35.63:2.8: MS omits mano here in error. It is included in the following round in this Sutta, and in both rounds of the next Sutta. Mano is found in the PTS and BJT editions, which I follow here.

root_text.sn35.63:1.2: Atha kho āyasmā migajālo yena bhagavā …pe…
root_text.sn35.63:1.3: ekamantaṁ nisinno kho āyasmā migajālo bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn35.63:1.5: Kittāvatā nu kho, bhante, ekavihārī hoti, kittāvatā ca pana sadutiyavihārī hotī”ti?
root_text.sn35.63:2.1: “Santi kho, migajāla, cakkhuviññeyyā rūpā iṭṭhā kantā manāpā piyarūpā kāmūpasaṁhitā rajanīyā.
root_text.sn35.63:2.2: Tañce bhikkhu abhinandati abhivadati ajjhosāya tiṭṭhati.
root_text.sn35.63:2.3: Tassa taṁ abhinandato abhivadato ajjhosāya tiṭṭhato uppajjati nandī.
root_text.sn35.63:2.6: Nandisaṁyojanasaṁyutto kho, migajāla, bhikkhu sadutiyavihārīti vuccati.
root_text.sn35.63:2.8: Santi kho, migajāla, jivhāviññeyyā rasā iṭṭhā kantā manāpā piyarūpā kāmūpasaṁhitā rajanīyā.
root_text.sn35.63:2.9: Tañce bhikkhu abhinandati abhivadati ajjhosāya tiṭṭhati.
root_text.sn35.63:2.10: Tassa taṁ abhinandato abhivadato ajjhosāya tiṭṭhato uppajjati nandī.
root_text.sn35.63:2.13: Nandisaṁyojanasaṁyutto kho, migajāla, bhikkhu sadutiyavihārīti vuccati.
root_text.sn35.63:2.14: Evaṁvihārī ca, migajāla, bhikkhu kiñcāpi araññavanapatthāni pantāni senāsanāni paṭisevati appasaddāni appanigghosāni vijanavātāni manussarāhasseyyakāni paṭisallānasāruppāni;
root_text.sn35.63:3.1: Santi ca kho, migajāla, cakkhuviññeyyā rūpā iṭṭhā kantā manāpā piyarūpā kāmūpasaṁhitā rajanīyā.
root_text.sn35.63:3.2: Tañce bhikkhu nābhinandati nābhivadati nājjhosāya tiṭṭhati.
root_text.sn35.63:3.3: Tassa taṁ anabhinandato anabhivadato anajjhosāya tiṭṭhato nandī nirujjhati.
root_text.sn35.63:3.6: Nandisaṁyojanavisaṁyutto kho, migajāla, bhikkhu ekavihārīti vuccati …pe…
root_text.sn35.63:3.7: santi ca kho, migajāla, jivhāviññeyyā rasā …pe…
root_text.sn35.63:3.8: santi ca kho, migajāla, manoviññeyyā dhammā iṭṭhā kantā manāpā piyarūpā kāmūpasaṁhitā rajanīyā.
root_text.sn35.63:3.9: Tañce bhikkhu nābhinandati nābhivadati nājjhosāya tiṭṭhati.
root_text.sn35.63:3.10: Tassa taṁ anabhinandato anabhivadato anajjhosāya tiṭṭhato nandī nirujjhati.
root_text.sn35.63:3.13: Nandisaṁyojanavippayutto kho, migajāla, bhikkhu ekavihārīti vuccati.
root_text.sn35.63:3.14: Evaṁvihārī ca, migajāla, bhikkhu kiñcāpi gāmante viharati ākiṇṇo bhikkhūhi bhikkhunīhi upāsakehi upāsikāhi rājūhi rājamahāmattehi titthiyehi titthiyasāvakehi.

translation_text.sn35.63:1.2: Then Venerable Migajāla went up to the Buddha …
translation_text.sn35.63:1.4: “Sir, they speak of one who lives alone.
translation_text.sn35.63:1.5: How is one who lives alone defined? And how is living with a partner defined?”
translation_text.sn35.63:2.1: “Migajāla, there are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing.
translation_text.sn35.63:2.2: If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them,
translation_text.sn35.63:2.5: When there’s lust there is yoking.
translation_text.sn35.63:2.6: A mendicant who is fettered by relishing is said to live with a partner.
translation_text.sn35.63:2.7: There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches …
translation_text.sn35.63:2.8: There are ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing.
translation_text.sn35.63:2.9: If a mendicant approves, welcomes, and keeps clinging to them,
translation_text.sn35.63:2.12: When there’s lust there is yoking.
translation_text.sn35.63:2.13: A mendicant who is fettered by relishing is said to live with a partner.
translation_text.sn35.63:2.14: A mendicant who lives like this is said to live with a partner, even if they frequent remote lodgings in the wilderness and the forest that are quiet and still, far from the madding crowd, remote from human settlements, and fit for retreat.
translation_text.sn35.63:2.17: For craving is their partner, and they haven’t given it up.
translation_text.sn35.63:2.18: That’s why they’re said to live with a partner.
translation_text.sn35.63:3.1: There are sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing.
translation_text.sn35.63:3.2: If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them,
translation_text.sn35.63:3.4: When there’s no relishing there’s no lust.
translation_text.sn35.63:3.5: When there’s no lust there’s no yoking.
translation_text.sn35.63:3.6: A mendicant who is not fettered by relishing is said to live alone.
translation_text.sn35.63:3.7: There are sounds … smells … tastes … touches …
translation_text.sn35.63:3.8: There are ideas known by the mind, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing.
translation_text.sn35.63:3.9: If a mendicant doesn’t approve, welcome, and keep clinging to them,
translation_text.sn35.63:3.11: When there’s no relishing there’s no lust.
translation_text.sn35.63:3.12: When there’s no lust there’s no yoking.
translation_text.sn35.63:3.13: A mendicant who is not fettered by relishing is said to live alone.
translation_text.sn35.63:3.14: A mendicant who lives like this is said to live alone, even if they live within a village crowded by monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen; by rulers and their chief ministers, and monastics of other religions and their disciples.
translation_text.sn35.63:3.17: For craving is their partner, and they have given it up.
translation_text.sn35.63:3.18: That’s why they’re said to live alone.”
